replies.author: @Knowbody42
replies.text:
​​​@Par-Blindspot Here's what I think of Disney's "Star Wars" trilogy:
TFA was basically a second rate attempt to re-make A New Hope, without the character development or real struggles to show that Rey actually earned her skills and power, mostly relying on nostalgia for the previous films to capture peoples emotions rather than earning it in its own right.

Its silver lining was that it set up a bunch of open questions that a skilled writer *could* have built upon.
JJ Abrams himself might have done a mediocre job of expanding on them if *he* made the second movie.

But instead, it was handed to Rian Johnson, who made it his mission to shut down every single one of those open questions in the most anticlimactic ways possible, while character assassinating Luke to turn him into some hopeless bum, which makes no sense given how he was in the original trilogy.
And what the hell was that Mary Poppins scene with Leia?

And that left them scrambling to come up with a way to continue the third movie. And what did they come up with? Some ridiculous plot with Palpatine somehow returning with a massive hidden fleet? It's like something a five year old would think of. There is no in-universe sense to it at all.

There was no coherent overarching story throughout the trilogy, many of the plot points made no real sense except to move the story along, the characters made all sorts of dumb decisions that made no sense for what their characters were, many of the characters were unlikeable (especially Holdo, and what they turned Luke into), Rey didn't truly earn her powers through genuine struggle and development, other characters that actually had potential were wasted (Finn, Hux).

If you're going to make a character make dumb decisions, at least do it in a way that makes sense for the character so that we can believe it's actually something that character would do, and not just a convenient way to progress the movie.

replies.text:
​​​​@Par-Blindspot You're missing the point entirely.
Anakin and Luke did not simply pick up a lightsaber and immediately win the fight on the first try.
Luke trained with Obi Wan, then Yoda, then went out to fight Darth Vader despite Yoda telling him he wasn't ready. And Yoda was right. Luke lost, and lost his hand in the process.
The second time Luke fought Vader, he wasn't up to the task either. Vader was winning without much effort, and Luke only survived because Vader let him live. He was testing Luke, and goading him to join the dark side.

How did Luke win in the end? Because he refused to join the dark side despite everything, even if it would cost him his life. And that convinced Vader to join the light and sacrifice himself to kill Palpatine. Did you notice Palpatine was killing Vader with force lightning? What do you think killed him?

Anakin wasn't the strongest all the time in the prequels, either. Grievous was stronger than him. Dooku was stronger than him the first time. And he wasn't mentally strong enough to avoid being manipulated.

Compare this to Rey. She picks up a lightsaber and wins on the first try against Kylo. She reverses his force mind reading with *zero* prior training. She fixes the Millenium Falcon in 2 seconds when Han Solo couldn't.
That's not character development. It's just bad writing.

You know, I floated this idea with Grok on the grounds that some works of art should be designated protected from cultural vandalism.  He pointed to real world examples of 'protected art' - even in the States.  It may yet come to pass that what KK did was a genuine crime against  art.  That it was 'only' 'low art' i.e. popular art (as opposed to elitist 'high art' such as opera and paintings by billionaire collectors) is immaterial as the feelings of injury inflicted upon the millions of fans worldwide is as real as any Frenchman would feel if someone desecrated the Mona Lisa.
One day I might try to get the ball rolling with a petition to introduce a Protection of Popular Art Bill that would create rules that would encompass culturally significant works of pop art such as Dr. Who (in the UK) that dictate that even the owner of the IP (intellectual property) cannot make additions to, or subtractions from, or in anyway otherwise harm the original protected art work while still be able to *attempt* to extend the property in case it can be improved upon.  If this took off in the UK, maybe it could inspire a similar movement in the States - the most influential country in the world.

replies.author: @theunknowncommenter725
replies.text:
@troylowe814 You still hanging on to this false narrative that Lucas was responsible for destroying the Star Wars franchise. He did not. 

The idea that ewoks were created solely for merchandising purposes is a narrative pushed by disgruntled producer Gary Kurtz.

This is false. In truth the creation of ewoks was more of an economic decision because it was too expensive at the time to make a full village of wookiee costumes, so they got the characters half size I made ewoks. Plus, they serve the storyline twofold: 1. They're part of Luke's journey as a Jedi because they combine two lessons he's learned from Obi-Wan and Yoda ("Your eyes can deceive you, don't trust them." and "Size matters Not.") so he knows not to underestimate them, and they're part of Lucas's fascination with primitive cultures with lesser weaponry overwhelming technological empires with superior forces. It was always about story. No matter what cynics say otherwise.

Even if the ewoks were created for merchandise purposes, at least Lucas was able to put a story before the dollar signs. That's why you will always see Lucas Star Wars merchandise sell and Disney Star Wars will never sell. Story came first.

That and we've actually had SIX great Star Wars movies before Lucas handed ownership over to Kathleen Kennedy. His only real mistake honestly. 

Kathleen Kennedy did not inherit a damaged franchise, seeing inherited a complicated one that beforehand could withstand criticism and people still cared about it. Not anymore.

This is so unlikely in our lifetimes as to be effectively impossible but one day, centuries into the future, when the Star Wars IP is ancient and some film historian discovers the remarkable OT, loves it enough to recognise the criminal nature of the cultural vandalism that was inflicted upon it under Disney,, will buy the rights and de-canonise Disney.  The moment that happens, even though no one who cares, or ever would have cared, may know about it, Hand Solo will be resurrected and fly off into the sunset to die peacefully as the heroic scoundrel we all know and love,  Luke Skywalker will be resurrected as the noble hero who would never give up and hide himself away, all the great potential new adventures will become possible again, and 'Rey Skywalker' will vanish like a bad smell in a fresh breeze.

replies.text: @maxmazzotti6651 new laws can be brought in e.g. cultural vandalism charges.  Millions of real people were emotionally wounded.  I grew up with a kind of boyish idolisation of Han Solo and had a giant Millennium Falcon cockpit poster on my bedroom wall from age 9 to about age 19, and I still feel a pang of sadness when I think that he was killed by his own son.  Then I think some more and see Luke and what they did to him, and Rey 'Skywalker' stealing his name, and I feel sick.  Consider that that sickness in my stomach must be similar to the sickness in the stomach that people feel thinking about Lesley Ann Downey being tortured to death my Ian Brady and Myra Hindley.  it's a genuine twisting of an emotional dagger.  No one should be allowed to crush dreams and childhood heroes and hurt people as KK (and JJ and RJ) did.
